content.text: is the technique of processing multiple training samples (a batch) simultaneously in parallel on the GPU rather than processing them one-by-one sequentially.

refers to the number of training examples processed at once before updating model weights. GPU processes the entire batch in parallel, leveraging its 16,000+ cores to compute operations on all samples simultaneously.
Why Batched Computation Matters in Generative AI
A larger batch size allows the GPU to process more data in parallel,

Batch Size = 64

GPU (16,000 cores)
├── Core 1     → Sample 1
├── Core 2     → Sample 2
├── Core 3     → Sample 3
├── ...        → ...
├── Core 64    → Sample 64
└── Core 65-16000 → Additional parallel operations (matrix math)
Instead of waiting for one task to finish before starting another, GPU executes
across all 64 samples in the batch.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Model
model = nn.Linear(784, 10)

# Batch size
batch_size = 64

# Create batch of data (all on GPU)
input_batch = torch.randn(batch_size, 784).to('cuda')

# GPU processes ALL 64 samples in parallel
output_batch = model(input_batch)  # Batched computation

# output_batch shape: [64, 10] (all predictions)
The GPU processes the entire batch simultaneously rather than sequentially.

import tensorflow as tf

# Model
model = tf.keras.Sequential([
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(256, activation='relu', input_shape=(784,)),
    tf.keras.layers.Dense(10)
])

# Batch of data
input_batch = tf.random.normal([64, 784])

# GPU processes batch in parallel
output_batch = model(input_batch, training=True)  # Batched computation
: Fully utilizes GPU, faster, but can cause overfitting and instability

GPU has limited pipelines (~12)

Batch Size < 12:  Partial utilization
Batch Size = 12:  Full utilization (saturated)
Batch Size > 12:  No additional speedup (already saturated)
A GPU can only do calculations in parallel
up to the number of pipelines it has
. Even with a batch size of one, there's plenty of opportunity for vectorization/parallelism.

Memory usage depends on:
- Batch size (bigger batch = more memory)
- Model size (larger model = more memory)
- Computation history (autograd stores gradients)
— if your model is large, autograd must store all computation history, and bigger batches use more memory.

# Optimal strategy for RTX 4090 (24GB VRAM)
# 1. Increase batch size to maximize parallel computation
batch_size = 256  # or higher if VRAM allows

# 2. Use multiple CUDA streams for different workloads
stream1 = torch.cuda.Stream()
stream2 = torch.cuda.Stream()

# 3. Overlap data transfers with computation
with stream1:
    input1 = data1.to('cuda')  # Transfer while computing
    output1 = model(input1)

with stream2:
    input2 = data2.to('cuda')  # Transfer while computing
    output2 = model(input2)

:
- Allows data to be copied from CPU to GPU while computations are ongoing
- Handles different workloads simultaneously

Balance the two:
- Running too many streams with large batch sizes → memory bottleneck
- Optimal: Find sweet spot between batch size and number of streams

Synchronous batching (bad):
CPU batch prep → GPU compute → Idle gap → CPU batch prep → GPU compute

Asynchronous continuous batching (good):
CPU prep batch 1
GPU compute batch 1
CPU prep batch 2 (while GPU computing)
GPU compute batch 2 (while CPU prep batch 3)

# Try different batch sizes and measure performance
for batch_size in [16, 32, 64, 128, 256]:
    input_batch = torch.randn(batch_size, 784).to('cuda')
    
    # Measure forward pass time
    start = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True)
    end = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True)
    
    start.record()
    output = model(input_batch)
    end.record()
    
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    time = start.elapsed_time(end)
    print(f"Batch size {batch_size}: {time}ms")

# Check available memory before large batch
if torch.cuda.get_device_properties(0).total_memory < needed_memory:
    batch_size = batch_size // 2  # Reduce batch size
    print(f"Reduced batch size to {batch_size}")
: Cooks one dish at a time
: Number of dishes on the order (64)
: Each worker cooks one dish part
: Prep next batch while cooking current batch (CUDA streams)
— processing 64 samples in one GPU pass takes roughly the same wall-clock time as processing 1 sample, giving near-linear throughput scaling with batch size up to the hardware saturation point.
— mini-batch gradients average out noise across samples, producing a smoother loss landscape and more stable convergence than stochastic (batch size 1) updates.
— GPUs have very high memory bandwidth (e.g., 2 TB/s on H100); batching keeps the bandwidth fully saturated with useful computation rather than wasting cycles on per-sample overhead.
— cuBLAS, cuDNN, and FlashAttention are all designed to operate on batched tensors; their throughput often degrades significantly for batch size 1.
— each sample in the batch requires activations and gradients to be stored; doubling batch size roughly doubles activation memory.
— very large batches (> 8192) converge to sharp minima that generalise worse than small-batch sharp minima; requires learning rate scaling (linear scaling rule: LR = base_LR × batch_size / 256).
— at inference time, batching increases throughput but also increases latency per request; real-time applications often need batch size 1.
— if sequences in a batch differ widely in length, short sequences are padded; for a very skewed length distribution, over 50% of compute can be wasted on padding tokens.
When to Use Large vs. Small Batches

Training and inference have opposite requirements. During training, use the largest batch that fits in VRAM — it maximises GPU utilisation and gradient stability. During real-time inference, prefer smaller batches (1–8) to minimise latency; for throughput-oriented inference (batch embedding, offline processing), use large batches.

When the target batch size does not fit in VRAM, gradient accumulation runs multiple small forward+backward passes and accumulates gradients before performing a single optimizer step. The effective batch size is
, and the optimizer sees the gradient as if the entire effective batch were processed at once.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.optim import AdamW

model = nn.TransformerDecoder(...)   # your model
optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)

MICRO_BATCH  = 4       # fits in VRAM
ACCUM_STEPS  = 32      # effective batch = 4 × 32 = 128
scaler = torch.cuda.amp.GradScaler()   # mixed precision

model.train()
optimizer.zero_grad()

for step, (x, y) in enumerate(dataloader):
    x, y = x.to("cuda"), y.to("cuda")

    # Forward + backward in mixed precision
    with torch.cuda.amp.autocast():
        loss = model(x, y) / ACCUM_STEPS    # normalise by accumulation steps

    scaler.scale(loss).backward()            # gradients accumulate

    if (step + 1) % ACCUM_STEPS == 0:
        scaler.step(optimizer)               # single optimizer step
        scaler.update()
        optimizer.zero_grad()                # clear gradients after update
        print(f"Step {step // ACCUM_STEPS}: loss = {loss.item() * ACCUM_STEPS:.4f}")

vLLM and TGI (Text Generation Inference) use a technique called continuous batching or PagedAttention to maximise GPU utilisation during LLM serving. Instead of waiting for a fixed batch to fill before starting, the serving system dynamically adds new requests to in-progress batch computations.

# Conceptual illustration of continuous batching scheduler
from dataclasses import dataclass
from collections import deque

@dataclass
class Request:
    id: int
    tokens: list[int]
    max_new_tokens: int = 256

def continuous_batching_scheduler(
    pending: deque[Request],
    running: list[Request],
    max_batch: int = 32,
) -> list[Request]:
    """
    At each decode step, add new requests to the running batch
    if capacity allows.  Completed requests are evicted.
    """
    # Evict finished requests
    running = [r for r in running if len(r.tokens) < r.max_new_tokens]

    # Add new requests up to capacity
    while pending and len(running) < max_batch:
        running.append(pending.popleft())

    return running

# In a real serving loop:
# while True:
#     running = continuous_batching_scheduler(pending, running)
#     next_tokens = model.forward_batch([r.tokens for r in running])
#     for r, tok in zip(running, next_tokens):
#         r.tokens.append(tok)

Continuous batching allows GPU utilisation to remain above 90% even with heterogeneous request arrival rates, compared to 40–60% for fixed static batching. This is why production LLM inference services (ChatGPT, Claude API) can serve thousands of concurrent users from a single GPU cluster.
Not scaling learning rate with batch size
— the linear scaling rule (LR ∝ batch_size) applies; using a fixed LR with a large batch diverges or converges slowly.
— PyTorch accumulates gradients by default; not zeroing leads to incorrect gradient estimates.
Padding all sequences to the longest in the dataset
— pad to the longest sequence in the batch, not the dataset; use dynamic padding in the DataCollator.
Testing with batch size 1 and assuming it scales
— some operations (LayerNorm, BatchNorm) behave differently at batch size 1; always test with representative batch sizes.

# WRONG — LR not scaled with batch size
big_batch_size = 512
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4)   # same LR as batch 32 — too small!

# CORRECT — linear scaling rule
base_lr = 3e-4
base_batch = 32
big_batch = 512
scaled_lr = base_lr * (big_batch / base_batch)   # 3e-4 * 16 = 4.8e-3
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=scaled_lr)

# Also add a warmup period when using large LR
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import LinearLR
warmup = LinearLR(optimizer, start_factor=0.01, end_factor=1.0, total_iters=100)
— reduces memory by 2× and increases throughput on Tensor Core GPUs (Volta and later) with negligible quality loss.
Sort sequences by length before batching
— reduces padding waste; group similarly-lengthed sequences together to minimise idle compute.
before optimising — identify whether the bottleneck is compute (GPU-bound) or memory transfer (memory-bound) before choosing a remedy.
for an automatic 1.5–3× speedup via kernel fusion, without changing your batch logic.

GPU throughput for matrix operations scales approximately linearly with batch size until saturation — after which adding more samples to the batch does not increase throughput because all CUDA cores are already occupied. The saturation point depends on the model size and GPU: for a 7B-parameter LLM on an A100, the prefill stage saturates at around batch size 16–32 for 2048-token sequences.
Halves memory, 2x throughput on Tensor Cores
Emulates large batch without extra VRAM
Yes — use when batch doesn't fit in VRAM
Splits model across GPUs to handle larger models
Yes — model parallelism, not batch parallelism
IO-efficient attention kernel, reduces memory IO
Yes — kernel optimisation, works on any batch size
— mixed-precision training; works with any batch size.
(PyTorch 2.0) — compiles the model to optimised CUDA kernels, giving 1.5–3× speedup.
— production LLM serving with continuous batching and PagedAttention; supports batch inference for OpenAI-compatible APIs.
— optimised inference engine with in-flight batching and weight quantisation.
— simplifies gradient accumulation and multi-GPU batching in a few lines of code.
— out-of-memory errors during training are the most common reliability issue; use try/except around the training step and halve batch size on OOM.

— GPU batch operations may produce slightly different results across runs due to floating-point accumulation order; set CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG=:4096:8 and use torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True) for reproducibility.
— clip gradients (torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_) to prevent instability when scaling batch size.
: Process multiple samples simultaneously in parallel on GPU
: Number of training examples processed at once
: GPU processes entire batch simultaneously, not sequentially
: Larger batch size increases GPU efficiency
: Maximizes GPU, fastest, but can overfit/unstable
: GPU saturates at ~12 pipelines, beyond that no speedup
: Bigger batch + large model = memory overflow

for matrix operations, making training large generative AI models practical.
